Authors: Peiwen Liu; Jibiao Zhang; Xiaozhong Ding; Yanxue Liu; Tingdong Li

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123446

Relevance: 0.590 (consider)

The crust–mantle interactions and geodynamic regimes of late Archean continental crust formation and evolution are a hot topic in global Precambrian research. As an important component of the early continental crust, tonalite, trondhjemite, and granodiorite (TTG) suites preserve key information on crust formation and evolution, but their origin remains a subject of intense debate. Here, we report zircon U Pb ages, major and trace element data, and Mo–Sr–Nd–Hf isotopic compositions for Mesoarchean TTGs and associated Nb-enriched basalts from the Kongling Complex, Yangtze Craton. The ~2.98 Ga Shuiyuesi TTGs are characterized by high Mg# values (47–55), high Sr/Y (58.4–63.9), and (La/Yb) N ratios (32.6–98.2), low Mo contents, low δ 98/95 Mo values (−0.69‰ to −0.52‰; relative to the NIST SRM 3134 standard), and depleted Sr–Nd–Hf isotopic signatures. These characteristics suggest that they were derived from the partial melting of a residual oceanic slab, which lost isotopically heavy Mo during dehydration. Similar to the Shuiyuesi TTGs, the ~2.95 Ga Maoya TTGs also show high Mg# values (44–57), high Sr/Y (46.7–118.6), and (La/Yb) N ratios (72.6–158.7). However, they exhibit higher δ 98/95 Mo (−0.13‰ to −0.04‰) values and more enriched Sr–Nd–Hf isotopic ratios than the Shuiyuesi TTGs, indicating that they were derived from the partial melting of ancient crustal source with heavy Mo isotopic compositions. The ~2.88 Ga Nb-enriched basalts are characterized by arc-type trace element signatures but display higher TiO 2 , P 2 O 5 , Nb, Nb/La, and Nb/U values than normal arc volcanics. They show low δ 98/95 Mo (−0.46‰ to −0.25‰) values and highly depleted Sr–Nd–Hf isotopic signatures, suggesting that they were formed by partial melting of a depleted mantle wedge metasomatized by melts derived from dehydrated oceanic crust. Considering the complex crust–mantle processes during the formation of TTGs and Nb-enriched basalts, we suggest that the Mesoarchean lithological assemblages in the Kongling Complex were most likely formed in an active continental margin setting. Our research also indicates that Mo isotopes can effectively trace the source of Archean TTG rocks as well as crust–mantle interactions.

item.title: Gagarinite-type Fe <sub>2.5</sub> (OH) <sub>6</sub> under the deep lower mantle conditions
item.dc:creator: Li Zhang
Ziqiang Yang
Ho-kwang Mao
item.prism:publicationName: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
item.description: Earth’s lower mantle is dominated by (Mg,Fe)SiO 3 bridgmanite. It was reported that (Mg,Fe)SiO 3 decomposes into an Fe-depleted bridgmanite phase and an iron-rich phase with a hexagonal structure (H-phase) under high pressure–temperature conditions of the deep lower mantle at depth >2,000 km. The nature of the decomposition reaction remains elusive due to the lack of information on the crystal chemistry of the H-phase. Using the multigrain method for high-pressure structure determination, here we reported in situ structure determination of the H-phase at 117 GPa and after temperature quench from 2,500 K. The structure analysis was performed by scaling and merging the single-crystal datasets of three selected grains. The crystal structure has been solved in space group P 6 3 / m , with a = 5.0708(2) Å and c = 2.8214(1) Å at 117 GP and 298 K. We obtained Fe 2.538 O 6 from the structure refinement and estimated the hydrogen content based on the volume expansion, suggesting a chemical formula Fe 2.5 (OH) 6 for the H-phase. The H-phase Fe 2.5 (OH) 6 resembles the crystal structure of the gagarinite-type minerals. To our knowledge, the gagarinite-type Fe 2.5 (OH) 6 is the most water-rich phase reported so far under the deep lower mantle conditions. We would expect that the gagarinite-type Fe 2.5 (OH) 6 is a potential water carrier in the deep lower mantle. Further, Fe-depletion in bridgmanite and formation of Fe 2.5 (OH) 6 may contribute to chemical heterogeneities in the bottom 1,000 km of the mantle and explain some of the complex seismic anomalies. Published: 2026-04-22T16:07:51+00:00 Venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences Score: 0.589 (consider)

Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

Earth’s lower mantle is dominated by (Mg,Fe)SiO 3 bridgmanite. The crystal structure has been solved in space group P 6 3 / m , with a = 5.0708(2) Å and c = 2.8214(1) Å at 117 GP and 298 K. The H-phase Fe 2.5 (OH) 6 resembles the crystal structure of the gagarinite-type minerals. To our knowledge, the gagarinite-type Fe 2.5 (OH) 6 is the most water-rich phase reported so far under the deep lower mantle conditions. We would expect that the gagarinite-type Fe 2.5 (OH) 6 is a potential water carrier in the deep lower mantle. Further, Fe-depletion in bridgmanite and formation of Fe 2.5 (OH) 6 may contribute to chemical heterogeneities in the bottom 1,000 km of the mantle and explain some of the complex seismic anomalies.

item.title: Silurian subduction of Proto-Tethys along the southern East Kunlun Orogen: Insight from geochronology and geochemistry of the Huxiaoqing ophiolitic mélange, northern Tibet plateau
item.dc:creator: Rutao Zang
Yunpeng Dong
Dengfeng He
Shengsi Sun
Bo Hui
Bin Zhang
Qingxing Luo
Fubao Chong
Zengshuai Zuo
Qiwen Dai
item.description: The tectonic evolution of the Proto-Tethys Ocean during the Late Silurian remains controversial, particularly regarding its closure versus ongoing subduction along the East Kunlun Orogenic Belt. The Huxiaoqing ophiolitic mélange provides key evidence for continued subduction. It exhibits a typical block-in-matrix structure with intense deformation. The matrix consists mainly of foliated meta -tuff and metasiltstone, enclosing a diverse range of blocks, including serpentinite, metabasalt, andesite, rhyolite, chert, and limestone. Geochemically, the serpentinites show low rare earth element (REE) contents and depletions in Nb, Ta, Zr, and Hf, indicating a subduction influence. The metabasalts exhibit a calc-alkaline affinity, characterized by flat REE patterns and Nb-Ta depletions, consistent with a volcanic arc setting. The andesites exhibit calc-alkaline characteristics, enrichment in light REE (LREE), depletion in heavy REE (HREE), and Nb-Ta depletion, supporting a continental arc environment. These features collectively indicate a subduction-related origin. Zircon U-Pb dating yields crystallization ages of 419–423 Ma for the andesite and rhyolite blocks, indicating that the mélange formed in the Late Silurian. The matrix yields a maximum depositional age of 485 Ma based on the youngest detrital zircon. Combined with regional data, these results indicate that the Proto-Tethys Ocean had been subducting along the East Kunlun Orogenic Belt since the Early Paleozoic, undergoing a prolonged and complex tectonic evolution. By the Late Silurian, the ocean had not yet fully closed, and subduction was still ongoing. Published: 2026-04-20T23:13:37+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.585 (consider)

The tectonic evolution of the Proto-Tethys Ocean during the Late Silurian remains controversial, particularly regarding its closure versus ongoing subduction along the East Kunlun Orogenic Belt. The Huxiaoqing ophiolitic mélange provides key evidence for continued subduction. It exhibits a typical block-in-matrix structure with intense deformation. The matrix consists mainly of foliated meta -tuff and metasiltstone, enclosing a diverse range of blocks, including serpentinite, metabasalt, andesite, rhyolite, chert, and limestone. Geochemically, the serpentinites show low rare earth element (REE) contents and depletions in Nb, Ta, Zr, and Hf, indicating a subduction influence. The metabasalts exhibit a calc-alkaline affinity, characterized by flat REE patterns and Nb-Ta depletions, consistent with a volcanic arc setting. The andesites exhibit calc-alkaline characteristics, enrichment in light REE (LREE), depletion in heavy REE (HREE), and Nb-Ta depletion, supporting a continental arc environment. These features collectively indicate a subduction-related origin. Zircon U-Pb dating yields crystallization ages of 419–423 Ma for the andesite and rhyolite blocks, indicating that the mélange formed in the Late Silurian. The matrix yields a maximum depositional age of 485 Ma based on the youngest detrital zircon. Combined with regional data, these results indicate that the Proto-Tethys Ocean had been subducting along the East Kunlun Orogenic Belt since the Early Paleozoic, undergoing a prolonged and complex tectonic evolution. By the Late Silurian, the ocean had not yet fully closed, and subduction was still ongoing.

item.title: Partially melted low velocity zones reveal an active upper mantle beneath Elysium Planitia, Mars
item.dc:creator: Mingwei Dai
Daoyuan Sun
Chenyang Zhao
Zhu Mao
Ningyu Sun
Huixing Bi
item.description: The current state of Mars’s interior is fundamental to understanding its thermal evolution, volcanic history, and potential habitability. While global-scale magmatism has declined since the Amazonian epoch, recent volcanism and concentrated seismicity in Elysium Planitia suggest localized, ongoing mantle activity, yet direct evidence has remained elusive. Here, we analyze InSight seismic data, combining receiver functions and PP/SS precursors to image crustal and upper mantle structures beneath three locations in Elysium Planitia. We identify consistent low-velocity zones at depths of ~70–100 km, marked by shear wave velocity reductions up to 36%. These anomalies are best explained by adiabatic decompression melting within a high-temperature upper mantle, with estimated melt fractions of 2–12%, potentially aided by the presence of water. Our results provide direct seismic evidence for partial melting and active dynamic mantle processes beneath Elysium Planitia, likely driven by a mantle plume, suggesting that Mars’s interior remains thermochemically active today. Seismic data from NASA’s InSight mission reveal partially molten zones in Mars’s upper mantle beneath Elysium Planitia, suggesting that a hot, active mantle plume is sustaining recent volcanism and seismic activity on Mars. Published: 2026-04-21T14:46:15+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.584 (consider)

Authors: Mingwei Dai; Daoyuan Sun; Chenyang Zhao; Zhu Mao; Ningyu Sun; Huixing Bi

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-72209-x

Relevance: 0.584 (consider)

The current state of Mars’s interior is fundamental to understanding its thermal evolution, volcanic history, and potential habitability. While global-scale magmatism has declined since the Amazonian epoch, recent volcanism and concentrated seismicity in Elysium Planitia suggest localized, ongoing mantle activity, yet direct evidence has remained elusive. Here, we analyze InSight seismic data, combining receiver functions and PP/SS precursors to image crustal and upper mantle structures beneath three locations in Elysium Planitia. We identify consistent low-velocity zones at depths of ~70–100 km, marked by shear wave velocity reductions up to 36%. These anomalies are best explained by adiabatic decompression melting within a high-temperature upper mantle, with estimated melt fractions of 2–12%, potentially aided by the presence of water. Our results provide direct seismic evidence for partial melting and active dynamic mantle processes beneath Elysium Planitia, likely driven by a mantle plume, suggesting that Mars’s interior remains thermochemically active today. Seismic data from NASA’s InSight mission reveal partially molten zones in Mars’s upper mantle beneath Elysium Planitia, suggesting that a hot, active mantle plume is sustaining recent volcanism and seismic activity on Mars.

item.title: Immiscibility of primary CO2-P2O5-rich silicate melt and the genesis of alkali basalts in the South China Sea
item.dc:creator: Hao Cheng
Guo-Liang Zhang
item.description: CO 2 and P 2 O 5 are preferentially extracted during low-degree of mantle melting based on the experimental studies. However, natural primary CO 2 -P 2 O 5 -rich melts have not been reported, and their evolutionary pathways and genetic relationships with other silicate melts remain poorly constrained. Here we report volcanic rocks from the Zhenbei seamount, South China Sea, characterized by whole‑rock CO 2 –P 2 O 5 enrichment, silicate‑poor compositions, and negative high‑field‑strength element (HFSE) anomalies that resemble those of carbonatites. We find co-existence of two types of volcanic glass in the rocks, CO 2 -P 2 O 5 -rich and alkali silicate-rich, respectively, indicating immiscibility occurred during magmatic processes. The CO 2 -P 2 O 5 -rich glass exhibits carbonatite-type trace element pattern, whereas the conjugate alkali silicate glass shows trace‑element characteristics comparable to global ocean‑island basalts (OIBs) and seamount basalts from the South China Sea. Results of in situ trace element analyses indicate HFSEs tend to partition into silicate melts, resulting in depletion of these elements in the conjugate CO 2 -P 2 O 5 -rich melts. Although the extreme P 2 O 5 enrichment preserving these textures is a localized phenomenon, the compositional trajectory of the immiscible silicate melts mirrors regional early-stage magmatic evolution. Ultimately, this rare preserved snapshot serves as a natural laboratory, demonstrating that liquid immiscibility is a physically viable mechanism for generating coeval carbonatite and alkaline silicate suites despite localized preservation conditions. Published: 2026-04-21T16:10:13+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.584 (consider)

CO 2 and P 2 O 5 are preferentially extracted during low-degree of mantle melting based on the experimental studies. Here we report volcanic rocks from the Zhenbei seamount, South China Sea, characterized by whole‑rock CO 2 –P 2 O 5 enrichment, silicate‑poor compositions, and negative high‑field‑strength element (HFSE) anomalies that resemble those of carbonatites. We find co-existence of two types of volcanic glass in the rocks, CO 2 -P 2 O 5 -rich and alkali silicate-rich, respectively, indicating immiscibility occurred during magmatic processes. The CO 2 -P 2 O 5 -rich glass exhibits carbonatite-type trace element pattern, whereas the conjugate alkali silicate glass shows trace‑element characteristics comparable to global ocean‑island basalts (OIBs) and seamount basalts from the South China Sea. Results of in situ trace element analyses indicate HFSEs tend to partition into silicate melts, resulting in depletion of these elements in the conjugate CO 2 -P 2 O 5 -rich melts. Ultimately, this rare preserved snapshot serves as a natural laboratory, demonstrating that liquid immiscibility is a physically viable mechanism for generating coeval carbonatite and alkaline silicate suites despite localized preservation conditions.

item.title: Glacial dysoxia in the deep subpolar North Atlantic during the Mid-Pleistocene Transition
item.dc:creator: Iván Hernández-Almeida
Francisco Javier Sierro
Gabriel M. Filippelli
Antje H. L. Voelker
Paula Diz
item.description: Abstract The transition from 41-kyr to 100-kyr climate cycles during the Mid-Pleistocene Transition (MPT; 1250–700 kyr), occurred in the absence of any significant shift in orbital forcing. The increase of carbon storage in the deep ocean between Marine Isotope Stage (MIS) 24–22 has been suggested as a main internal factor leading to the transition to 100-kyr glacial cycles. We present sedimentary redox proxies and benthic foraminifera assemblages that demonstrate persistent dysoxic conditions during the MPT at IODP Site U1314 (subpolar North Atlantic). During glacials between 940 and 870 kyr, benthic foraminifera species typical of high porewater oxygen concentration disappeared, and concentrations of manganese oxides and reactive phosphorus mineral phases, both influenced by redox state, showed reductions exceeding 50%. Here, we show that higher freshwater supply associated with ice-rafted delivery caused a reduction in deep-water convection, decreasing bottom-water oxygenation and favoring carbon storage in the subpolar North Atlantic during the MPT. Published: 2026-04-24T09:03:03+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.576 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Iván Hernández-Almeida; Francisco Javier Sierro; Gabriel M. Filippelli; Antje H. L. Voelker; Paula Diz

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-71268-4

Relevance: 0.576 (consider)

Abstract The transition from 41-kyr to 100-kyr climate cycles during the Mid-Pleistocene Transition (MPT; 1250–700 kyr), occurred in the absence of any significant shift in orbital forcing. The increase of carbon storage in the deep ocean between Marine Isotope Stage (MIS) 24–22 has been suggested as a main internal factor leading to the transition to 100-kyr glacial cycles. We present sedimentary redox proxies and benthic foraminifera assemblages that demonstrate persistent dysoxic conditions during the MPT at IODP Site U1314 (subpolar North Atlantic). During glacials between 940 and 870 kyr, benthic foraminifera species typical of high porewater oxygen concentration disappeared, and concentrations of manganese oxides and reactive phosphorus mineral phases, both influenced by redox state, showed reductions exceeding 50%. Here, we show that higher freshwater supply associated with ice-rafted delivery caused a reduction in deep-water convection, decreasing bottom-water oxygenation and favoring carbon storage in the subpolar North Atlantic during the MPT.

item.title: Spatially coupled CO2 activation and hydrogenation sites in 1 T′-MoS2 enable near-unity methanol selectivity
item.dc:creator: Yi Zhao
Bifa Ji
Jing Xu
Xuan Tang
Yongping Zheng
Chengsi Pan
Jiawei Zhang
Wangcheng Zhan
Yongfa Zhu
Yang Lou
item.description: The development of efficient catalysts for CO2 hydrogenation to methanol is highly urgent but is hindered by maintaining precise control over intermediate transfer. Here we demonstrate that the oxidized 2D 1 T′-MoS2 uniquely integrates both CO2 activation and selective hydrogenation functions within a single material through its inherent 2D structural merit. The singular S-edge structure of 1 T′-MoS2 creates a uniform catalytic landscape where in-plane O-substituted sulfur defects and oxidized edges operate in concert. This spatially organized system achieves CO2 conversion of 23.0% with a methanol selectivity of 99.2% and the specific reaction rate reaches 0.91 ± 0.01 gmethanol/gcat/h at 210 °C. We reveal the complete reaction trajectory: (i) preferential CO2 dissociation at in-plane sites generates weakly adsorbed *CO intermediates that (ii) undergo directed desorption-retrapping to edge sites where (iii) the oxidized edge of 1 T′ phase uniquely stabilizes the C-O bond during hydrogenation. This work establishes the phase-engineered 1 T′-MoS2 as a paradigm for single-material tandem catalysis to demonstrate how the spatially coupled active sites boost the CO2 hydrogenation. Improving catalysts for CO₂-to-methanol conversion is limited by poor control of reaction intermediates. Here oxidized 2D 1 T′-MoS2 couples activation and hydrogenation sites to enable high methanol selectivity. Published: 2026-04-21T17:54:45+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.569 (consider)

Relevance: 0.569 (consider)

The development of efficient catalysts for CO2 hydrogenation to methanol is highly urgent but is hindered by maintaining precise control over intermediate transfer. Here we demonstrate that the oxidized 2D 1 T′-MoS2 uniquely integrates both CO2 activation and selective hydrogenation functions within a single material through its inherent 2D structural merit. The singular S-edge structure of 1 T′-MoS2 creates a uniform catalytic landscape where in-plane O-substituted sulfur defects and oxidized edges operate in concert. This spatially organized system achieves CO2 conversion of 23.0% with a methanol selectivity of 99.2% and the specific reaction rate reaches 0.91 ± 0.01 gmethanol/gcat/h at 210 °C. We reveal the complete reaction trajectory: (i) preferential CO2 dissociation at in-plane sites generates weakly adsorbed *CO intermediates that (ii) undergo directed desorption-retrapping to edge sites where (iii) the oxidized edge of 1 T′ phase uniquely stabilizes the C-O bond during hydrogenation. This work establishes the phase-engineered 1 T′-MoS2 as a paradigm for single-material tandem catalysis to demonstrate how the spatially coupled active sites boost the CO2 hydrogenation. Improving catalysts for CO₂-to-methanol conversion is limited by poor control of reaction intermediates. Here oxidized 2D 1 T′-MoS2 couples activation and hydrogenation sites to enable high methanol selectivity.

item.title: Deciphering grain-size reduction as a driver of mid-lithosphere discontinuity formation
item.dc:creator: Mingqi Liu
Taras V. Gerya
Zhong-Hai Li
Ling Chen
James A. D. Connolly
item.description: The mid-lithosphere discontinuity (MLD) is a fundamental yet enigmatic seismic boundary within Earth’s lithosphere, whose origin remains debated despite decades of study. Using thermomechanical models with self-consistent grain-size evolution, we propose a previously unrecognized mechanism for MLD formation. Plate deformation induces pronounced grain-size reduction within the brittle-ductile transition, generating permeability barriers that trap ascending fluids. These fluid-rich layers reproduce the observed seismic velocity drops. Their depth scales with lithospheric thickness and thermal structure and is fine-tuned by rheology evolution through brittle-ductile partitioning. The interplay between grain-size reduction and fluid accumulation imparts dual mechanical behavior to the MLD, stabilizing it within cold, ancient lithosphere or enabling localized failure under thermal perturbation. This framework identifies grain-scale processes as the primary control on MLD formation and links mantle-lithosphere coupling to planetary lithospheric evolution. Published: 2026-04-24T17:58:32+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.568 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mingqi Liu; Taras V. Gerya; Zhong-Hai Li; Ling Chen; James A. D. Connolly

Journal: Science Advances

DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.aed4229

Relevance: 0.568 (consider)

The mid-lithosphere discontinuity (MLD) is a fundamental yet enigmatic seismic boundary within Earth’s lithosphere, whose origin remains debated despite decades of study. Using thermomechanical models with self-consistent grain-size evolution, we propose a previously unrecognized mechanism for MLD formation. Plate deformation induces pronounced grain-size reduction within the brittle-ductile transition, generating permeability barriers that trap ascending fluids. These fluid-rich layers reproduce the observed seismic velocity drops. Their depth scales with lithospheric thickness and thermal structure and is fine-tuned by rheology evolution through brittle-ductile partitioning. The interplay between grain-size reduction and fluid accumulation imparts dual mechanical behavior to the MLD, stabilizing it within cold, ancient lithosphere or enabling localized failure under thermal perturbation. This framework identifies grain-scale processes as the primary control on MLD formation and links mantle-lithosphere coupling to planetary lithospheric evolution.

item.title: Subduction Initiation Volcanism Leading to Development of the Talkeetna Arc, Lower Shuyak Formation, Kodiak Archipelago, Alaska
item.dc:creator: B. M. Keough
M. P. Eddy
K. D. Ridgway
W. Crain
item.description: Abstract Our understanding of subduction initiation remains limited by the difficulty of making direct observations of geologically recent examples. In the geologic record, supra‐subduction zone ophiolites are often interpreted to record syn‐initiation slab rollback and upper plate extension. Unfortunately, many ophiolites are decoupled from records of subsequent arc maturation and/or lack robust geochronological constraints. In this contribution, we investigate the initiation of the Late Triassic‐Jurassic Talkeetna Arc of southern Alaska. The Talkeetna Arc is one of the world's preeminent examples of an oceanic magmatic arc, yet the timing and tectonic record of its initiation remain unconstrained. Our study integrates geologic mapping with a chemostratigraphic analysis of ∼3.5 km of pillow basalts that comprise the Lower Shuyak Formation (Kodiak Archipelago, Alaska). The Lower Shuyak Formation hosts Late Triassic calc‐alkaline plutons and is overlain by ∼2 km of volcaniclastic strata. Initial mafic volcanism was characterized by high‐Ti, E‐MORB‐like tholeiites that are enriched in high field strength elements (HFSEs) and light rare earth elements. Compositions transition up section to HFSE/LREE‐depleted island arc tholeiites that become enriched in Th. These trends are consistent with petrologic processes during subduction initiation. High‐precision U‐Pb zircon dates (CA‐ID‐TIMS) constrain initiation to ∼232 Ma or slightly earlier. Our results demonstrate that initiation of the Talkeetna Arc occurred earlier than previous estimates, slightly preceding the eruption of regionally extensive flood basalts interpreted by previous studies as plume‐related. This relationship may suggest that subduction initiation was plume‐induced, or that rifting of the upper plate was the primary driver of basaltic volcanism regionally. Published: 2026-04-21T06:35:03+00:00 Venue: Tectonics Score: 0.566 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: B. M. Keough; M. P. Eddy; K. D. Ridgway; W. Crain

Journal: Tectonics

DOI: 10.1029/2026tc009378

Relevance: 0.566 (consider)

Abstract Our understanding of subduction initiation remains limited by the difficulty of making direct observations of geologically recent examples. In the geologic record, supra‐subduction zone ophiolites are often interpreted to record syn‐initiation slab rollback and upper plate extension. Unfortunately, many ophiolites are decoupled from records of subsequent arc maturation and/or lack robust geochronological constraints. In this contribution, we investigate the initiation of the Late Triassic‐Jurassic Talkeetna Arc of southern Alaska. The Talkeetna Arc is one of the world's preeminent examples of an oceanic magmatic arc, yet the timing and tectonic record of its initiation remain unconstrained. Our study integrates geologic mapping with a chemostratigraphic analysis of ∼3.5 km of pillow basalts that comprise the Lower Shuyak Formation (Kodiak Archipelago, Alaska). The Lower Shuyak Formation hosts Late Triassic calc‐alkaline plutons and is overlain by ∼2 km of volcaniclastic strata. Initial mafic volcanism was characterized by high‐Ti, E‐MORB‐like tholeiites that are enriched in high field strength elements (HFSEs) and light rare earth elements. Compositions transition up section to HFSE/LREE‐depleted island arc tholeiites that become enriched in Th. These trends are consistent with petrologic processes during subduction initiation. High‐precision U‐Pb zircon dates (CA‐ID‐TIMS) constrain initiation to ∼232 Ma or slightly earlier. Our results demonstrate that initiation of the Talkeetna Arc occurred earlier than previous estimates, slightly preceding the eruption of regionally extensive flood basalts interpreted by previous studies as plume‐related. This relationship may suggest that subduction initiation was plume‐induced, or that rifting of the upper plate was the primary driver of basaltic volcanism regionally.

item.title: Comparative Analysis of Seismicity Across Three Depth‐Dependent Slip Regimes in the Japan Trench Subduction Zone
item.dc:creator: Yuta Ito
Tomoaki Nishikawa
Yoshihiro Ito
Shukei Ohyanagi
Hiroyuki Noda
item.description: Abstract Understanding the cause of spatial variations in seismicity is crucial for comprehending the physics governing earthquake activity. Off Iwate, in the northern Japan Trench subduction zone, the plate boundary can be divided into three distinct zones based on depth‐dependent slip regimes: the slow earthquake, asperity, and stable creeping zones. We statistically compare their seismicity characteristics. Our results demonstrate that the slow earthquake zone produces significantly more aftershocks, whereas the asperity zone exhibits the lowest seismic activity. Conversely, the stable creeping zone has the highest rate of background seismicity. We interpret these distinct characteristics based on the respective broad‐scale slip behaviors. Moreover, we identify a clear kink in the cumulative magnitude‐frequency distribution within the stable creeping zone. Calculations of rupture arrest probability suggest a characteristic rupture size of M 3.7 (∼1 km scale) in this zone. The proposed rupture arrest probability may prove useful for future studies on earthquake source physics. Published: 2026-04-22T15:02:03+00:00 Venue: Geophysical Research Letters Score: 0.566 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuta Ito; Tomoaki Nishikawa; Yoshihiro Ito; Shukei Ohyanagi; Hiroyuki Noda

Journal: Geophysical Research Letters

DOI: 10.1029/2025gl120861

Relevance: 0.566 (consider)

Abstract Understanding the cause of spatial variations in seismicity is crucial for comprehending the physics governing earthquake activity. Off Iwate, in the northern Japan Trench subduction zone, the plate boundary can be divided into three distinct zones based on depth‐dependent slip regimes: the slow earthquake, asperity, and stable creeping zones. We statistically compare their seismicity characteristics. Our results demonstrate that the slow earthquake zone produces significantly more aftershocks, whereas the asperity zone exhibits the lowest seismic activity. Conversely, the stable creeping zone has the highest rate of background seismicity. We interpret these distinct characteristics based on the respective broad‐scale slip behaviors. Moreover, we identify a clear kink in the cumulative magnitude‐frequency distribution within the stable creeping zone. Calculations of rupture arrest probability suggest a characteristic rupture size of M 3.7 (∼1 km scale) in this zone. The proposed rupture arrest probability may prove useful for future studies on earthquake source physics.
